items.title: Clarification surfaces a faster solution
items.scenario: Problem stated as 'find the k most frequent words in a stream'.
items.explanation: Two clarifying questions ('is the alphabet bounded?', 'is the stream finite or unbounded?') turn the problem from 'sort a billion words' (impossible) into 'min-heap of size k' (trivial) or 'count-min sketch' (production-grade).

items.title: Pacing failure
items.scenario: You spend 35 minutes refining the approach; coding starts at minute 40.
items.explanation: Even with a brilliant approach, the interviewer can't evaluate code quality from half a function. A timer on each phase saves the mock.

items.title: Recovery from a wrong path
items.scenario: You're 15 minutes into a brute-force solution that won't fit the constraints; the interviewer hints at a hash table.
items.explanation: Strong candidates say 'thanks, let me restart with a hash table' and lose 5 minutes. Weak candidates push on with the wrong approach to avoid 'losing'. Mocks rehearse the comfortable retreat.

items.title: Edge cases self-surfaced
items.scenario: Implementing binary search; you say out loud 'I'll handle empty array, single element, target outside range'.
items.explanation: Listing edge cases *before* the interviewer asks is a strong signal. Mock interviews let you build this reflex without paying the cost of missing it in a real loop.

items.title: Behavioural rehearsal
items.scenario: 'Tell me about a time you disagreed with a teammate on a technical decision.'
items.explanation: A short, structured STAR (Situation/Task/Action/Result) story rehearsed in a mock is dramatically tighter than the same story improvised cold.
